questions.question: An economy produces 400 drones or 800 tractors. Drone production becomes less efficient, reducing maximum drones to 320 while tractors stay at 800. What is the new opportunity cost of 1 drone?
options: 2 tractors, the original opportunity cost before the efficiency reduction
2.5 tractors, the new ratio of maximum tractors to maximum drone output
0.4 drones per tractor, the inverse of the tractor-to-drone ratio now
320 tractors, representing the total drone production capacity lost
questions.explanation: New opportunity cost of 1 drone = maximum tractors ÷ maximum drones = $800 \div 320 = 2.5$ tractors sacrificed per drone produced.

questions.question: Country A requires 4 hours for Textiles and 8 hours for Wine. Country B requires 6 hours for Textiles and 12 hours for Wine. Which country holds the absolute advantage in both goods?
options: Country B, because its production times for both goods are closer together
Country A, because it has lower unit labor requirements for both Textiles and Wine
Country B, because its opportunity costs are identical to Country A's costs
Neither country, because both produce both goods using different labor hours
questions.explanation: Country A requires fewer hours for Textiles ($4 < 6$) and Wine ($8 < 12$), giving it the absolute advantage in both goods.

questions.question: Using the same data (A: 4T, 8W; B: 6T, 12W), what is the basis for trade between these two countries?
options: Country A should export Textiles because it has the absolute advantage in both goods
There is no basis for trade because both countries have identical opportunity costs
Country B should export Wine because it uses more labor hours in production
Trade should occur with Country A exporting Wine and Country B exporting Textiles
questions.explanation: Both have identical internal opportunity costs: 1 Textile = 0.5 Wine for both ($4/8 = 6/12 = 0.5$). Without different relative costs, there are no gains from trade.

questions.question: An economy's Production Possibility Frontier is drawn as a perfect straight line. What does this imply about the factors of production?
options: Resources are highly specialized and difficult to move between different industries
Factors of production are perfect substitutes and can be shifted with no efficiency loss
The economy is operating at a point located far inside its production frontier
Opportunity costs increase sharply as the economy shifts production toward one good
questions.explanation: A linear PPF indicates constant opportunity costs. Resources are equally productive in both sectors, so shifting them involves no change in marginal productivity.

questions.question: In Ricardian autarky, $P_X/P_Y = 3$ and $a_{LX} = 12$. What is the unit labor requirement for Good Y ($a_{LY}$)?
options: 36, calculated by multiplying the price ratio by the labor requirement of X
4, solved from the autarky price identity $P_X/P_Y = a_{LX}/a_{LY}$
12, matching the unit labor requirement for producing Good X
3, equal to the relative price ratio regardless of labor requirements
questions.explanation: In autarky, $P_X/P_Y = a_{LX}/a_{LY}$. So $3 = 12 / a_{LY}$, which gives $a_{LY} = 12/3 = 4$.

questions.question: World Relative Demand is very high and intersects the RS curve on its upper vertical segment. What is the specialization outcome?
options: Only the country with the lower opportunity cost will fully specialize in production
Both countries will fully specialize in their respective comparative advantage goods
Neither country will specialize because the high price benefits both equally
Production will split equally between both goods in each of the two trading nations
questions.explanation: When the world price lies above both countries' opportunity costs, both maximize profits by specializing completely in their comparative advantage good.

questions.question: In a many-good Ricardian model, how is the cutoff good that separates exports from imports determined?
options: By the absolute productivity level in each sector averaged across all countries
At the point where the relative productivity ratio equals the relative wage ratio
By consumer preference rankings, with the most popular goods exported first
By the alphabetical order of goods as listed in official trade classification schedules
questions.explanation: Home produces goods where $A_i/A^*_i > w/w^*$; Foreign produces where $A_i/A^*_i < w/w^*$. The cutoff is where the two ratios are exactly equal.

questions.question: Home productivity is 10 times Foreign's, but Home wages are 20 times higher. How will Home compete in international trade?
options: Home will export all goods because its absolute productivity is much higher everywhere
Home will only export goods where its productivity advantage exceeds the 20-to-1 wage ratio
Home will import all goods because its wage disadvantage is too large to overcome
Trade cannot occur because the wage gap must exactly match the productivity gap
questions.explanation: To be competitive, Home needs $A/A^* > w/w^*$. With wages 20 times higher, Home must find goods where its productivity lead exceeds $20:1$.

questions.question: Critics argue that trade exploits low-wage workers abroad. What is the standard economic rebuttal to this claim?
options: Low-wage workers are never affected by trade because they only produce for domestic markets
While wages are low, their real purchasing power typically rises compared to autarky conditions
International law requires all countries to pay identical minimum wages to their workers
Trade always reduces wages in both high-wage and low-wage countries equally
questions.explanation: Trade increases demand for their labor (raising employment and wages relative to autarky) and provides access to cheaper imported goods, improving real living standards.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, what is the fundamental nature of the gains from international trade?
options: Trade allows countries to extract monopoly profits from their trading partners
Trade acts as a more efficient method of production, indirectly producing imports with less labor
Trade primarily benefits governments through the collection of tariff revenue at borders
Trade gains come entirely from exploiting differences in nominal wage rates across countries
questions.explanation: Producing an export and trading it for an import is effectively an indirect way of producing the import using less labor than domestic production would require.

questions.question: A country opens to trade and has a comparative disadvantage in Good Y. Under the Ricardian model, what happens to domestic production of Good Y?
options: Production of Good Y stays constant because domestic consumers still demand it
Production of Good Y drops to zero as the country fully specializes in its advantage good
Production of Good Y increases because trade boosts demand for all domestically produced goods
Production of Good Y declines gradually as workers slowly retrain for other industries
questions.explanation: Under constant returns and perfect competition, the country fully specializes in its comparative advantage good. All labor shifts to that sector, and production of the disadvantaged good ceases entirely.

questions.question: What does the Utility Possibility Frontier represent in welfare economics?
options: The set of all possible production combinations available to the economy
The maximum utility one individual can achieve given the utility level of another individual
The initial distribution of utilities before any trade or exchange takes place
The point where all individuals in society have exactly equal levels of utility
questions.explanation: The UPF is the utility-space equivalent of the PPF. Every point on its boundary is Pareto Efficient, showing the trade-off between different individuals' welfare.

questions.question: Why is trade typically described as a potential Pareto Improvement rather than an actual one?
options: Trade benefits are purely theoretical and have never been observed in the real world
Total gains are positive, but domestic losers from trade are rarely fully compensated
Free trade always makes every single citizen in every country strictly better off
International law prohibits governments from compensating workers displaced by imports
questions.explanation: While the economic pie grows larger, import-competing workers and capital owners often lose. Without actual compensation, trade is only a potential improvement, not an actual one.

questions.question: International labor migration increases in the Ricardian model. What happens to total world output?
options: World output decreases because migration creates social and political instability
World output increases as labor moves to countries where its marginal product is higher
World output remains constant because total global labor supply has not changed
World output rises in destination countries but falls by the same amount in source countries
questions.explanation: Labor moves from low-productivity countries to high-productivity countries. The same workers produce more output, increasing total global efficiency and world production.

questions.question: In a Ricardian model with transportation costs, when does a good become non-traded?
options: When the government explicitly bans the import or export of that particular good
When the productivity advantage is smaller than the cost of shipping the good internationally
When consumer preferences strongly favor domestically produced versions of the good
All goods are always traded regardless of transportation costs in the Ricardian model
questions.explanation: If the efficiency gain from comparative advantage is less than the cost of transporting the good, it is cheaper to produce and consume it locally. The good becomes non-traded.

questions.question: The relative wage is $w/w^* = 2$ and the relative productivity for Good X is $A_X/A^*_X = 2$. What is the trade status of Good X?
options: Only Home will produce Good X because its productivity is twice as high
Good X can be produced in either country because unit labor costs are exactly equal
Only Foreign will produce Good X because its wages are lower than Home's wages
Neither country will produce Good X because the ratios are too close to determine
questions.explanation: When $A_X/A^*_X = w/w^* = 2$, Home's productivity advantage is exactly offset by its higher wage. Unit labor costs are identical, and the good can be produced in either country.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, trade causes the price of the import-competing good to fall. What happens to the specific factors in that sector?
options: Their real income rises because cheaper imports increase their overall purchasing power
Both nominal and real income decline because returns are tied to the falling output price
Their nominal income falls but real income rises because all consumer prices decrease
Their income remains stable because factor returns are set by long-term fixed contracts
questions.explanation: Nominal returns are tied to the price of the good produced. Since factors cannot move to the export sector, they absorb the full price decline in both nominal and real terms.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, which group has the strongest incentive to lobby for a protective tariff?
options: Export sector workers who want the government to promote their products abroad
Both capital and labor currently employed together in the import-competing sector
Domestic consumers who benefit from the lower prices that free trade would bring
Foreign producers seeking guaranteed access to the protected domestic market
questions.explanation: Because both factors are trapped in the import-competing industry and cannot relocate, their entire economic welfare depends on the protected price of their sector's output.

questions.question: How do the production efficiency gains from trade in the immobile factor model compare to those in the Ricardian model?
options: The immobile factor model predicts larger production efficiency gains than the Ricardian one
There are no production efficiency gains in the immobile model because factors cannot reallocate
Both models predict identical production efficiency gains from opening to international trade
Production efficiency gains occur only in the export sector in the immobile factor model
questions.explanation: Without factor mobility, resources cannot shift to the comparative advantage sector. Gains from trade in the immobile model come only from consumption efficiency, not from production reallocation.

questions.question: According to the Heckscher-Ohlin theorem, which goods will a country export?
options: Goods that use the country's relatively scarce factors most intensively
Goods that intensively use the factors of production the country possesses in abundance
Goods where the country has an absolute productivity advantage over its trading partners
The pattern of exports cannot be predicted by factor endowments in the H-O model
questions.explanation: The H-O theorem states that a country's comparative advantage lies in goods that make intensive use of its relatively abundant factors of production.

questions.question: Country A is capital-abundant and opens to trade. What happens to the wage-to-rental ratio ($w/r$)?
options: The ratio rises because trade benefits labor more than capital in all countries
The ratio decreases as capital's return rises and labor's return falls with specialization
The ratio remains constant because factor endowments are fixed in the short run
The ratio converges upward to match the level in labor-abundant trading partners
questions.explanation: Trade expands the capital-intensive sector in the capital-abundant country. Demand for capital rises ($r$ increases) while demand for labor falls relatively ($w$ falls), so $w/r$ decreases.

questions.question: According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, what effect does trade have on income inequality in a skill-abundant developed country?
options: Trade reduces inequality by raising wages for all workers across the entire economy
Trade widens the gap between skilled labor (abundant factor) and unskilled labor (scarce factor)
Trade has no effect on income distribution because all factors benefit equally
Trade narrows the gap because it increases competition from low-wage foreign workers
questions.explanation: In a skill-abundant country, trade raises returns to skilled labor (the abundant factor) and lowers returns to unskilled labor (the scarce factor), widening the income gap between them.

questions.question: A country's labor force grows significantly due to demographic changes. According to the Rybczynski Theorem, what happens to capital-intensive output?
options: Capital-intensive output expands because more workers are available for all sectors
Capital-intensive output contracts as resources are drawn into the expanding labor-intensive sector
Output of both goods expands proportionally because the entire economy grows larger
Capital-intensive output is unaffected because it depends only on the capital stock size
questions.explanation: The expanding labor-intensive sector draws capital away from the other sector. At constant goods prices, the capital-intensive industry actually contracts in absolute terms.

questions.question: $P_M$ and $P_A$ both increase by exactly 20% in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the allocation of labor?
options: Labor shifts entirely to Manufacturing because those goods are more capital-intensive
The allocation of labor between the two sectors remains completely unchanged
Labor shifts to Agriculture because food is a necessity with highly inelastic demand
Labor moves to whichever sector had the larger initial share of total employment
questions.explanation: Both labor demand curves shift up by the same 20%. The nominal wage rises by 20%, but the relative incentive to switch between sectors is unchanged. Labor allocation stays the same.

questions.question: $P_M$ increases in the Specific Factor Model while wages hypothetically stay constant. What happens to the nominal rental rate of Manufacturing Capital?
options: The rental rate decreases because higher output prices attract new competitors
The rental rate increases because $R_K = P_M \times MPK_M$, and $P_M$ is now higher
The rental rate remains constant because capital returns are fixed by global markets
The rental rate initially rises but returns to normal as labor moves into the sector
questions.explanation: $R_K = P_M \times MPK_M$. If $P_M$ rises while the amounts of labor and capital are unchanged, the Value of Marginal Product of Capital rises, increasing the nominal rental rate.

questions.question: Why do diminishing marginal returns to labor in the Specific Factor Model cause wages to rise by less than a single sector's price increase?
options: Government wage controls prevent nominal wages from fully adjusting to price changes
As labor moves into the expanding sector, each additional worker produces less, dampening wage growth
Wages are determined solely by the price of the other, constant-price good in the model
Diminishing returns only affect capital, not labor, in the Specific Factor framework
questions.explanation: As labor shifts into the sector with the rising price, the fixed specific factor means each additional worker adds less to output. This limits how much the nominal wage can rise.

questions.question: A country's specialized wine-growing climate is a specific factor. If the world price of wine falls, what happens to the rents earned by vineyard owners?
options: Rents fall by exactly the same percentage as the decline in the world wine price
Rents fall by more than the price decline, amplified by the Magnification Effect
Rents remain constant because the specific factor cannot be moved or repurposed
Rents rise because lower prices increase the quantity of wine demanded globally
questions.explanation: The Magnification Effect means the return to the specific factor changes more than proportionally to the price change in its sector. A price decline causes an even larger decline in rents.

questions.question: Why are internal economies of scale fundamentally inconsistent with the model of perfect competition?
options: Perfect competition requires that all firms earn large and persistent economic profits
Falling average costs give larger firms cost advantages, pushing the market toward monopoly or oligopoly
Internal economies require government ownership of all production facilities to function
Perfect competition and internal economies are actually fully compatible with each other
questions.explanation: With internal economies, larger firms have lower costs and can underprice smaller rivals. This drives the market toward concentration, which violates the price-taking assumption of perfect competition.

questions.question: In the Gravity Model of international trade, what is the relationship between trade volume and the distance separating two countries?
options: Trade volume increases with distance because countries seek diverse faraway products
Trade volume is negatively related to distance, which proxies for transport and other costs
Distance has no measurable effect on trade volume between any two countries
Trade volume is positively related to distance for goods but negative for services
questions.explanation: Distance captures transport costs, delivery time, and cultural differences. The Gravity Model predicts that trade diminishes as the distance between two countries increases.

questions.question: A natural industry cluster like Silicon Valley is a real-world example of what economic phenomenon?
options: Internal economies of scale where each firm grows very large to capture the market
External economies of scale, where firms benefit from shared labor pools and knowledge spillovers
Constant returns to scale where firm size has no effect on average production costs
A natural monopoly where only one firm can efficiently serve the entire global market
questions.explanation: External economies arise from industry concentration. All firms in the cluster benefit from specialized workers, suppliers, and informal knowledge exchange, regardless of their individual size.

questions.question: For which type of country is the optimal tariff always exactly zero?
options: A large country that can significantly influence world market prices
A small country that cannot affect world prices through its own trade policy
A developing country with infant industries that need temporary protection
Any country, because free trade is always the optimal policy regardless of size
questions.explanation: A small country faces perfectly elastic foreign supply. It cannot improve its terms of trade, so any positive tariff creates only deadweight losses with no offsetting benefit.

questions.question: Why do economists generally prefer a production subsidy over a tariff to support a domestic industry?
options: Subsidies always cost the government less money than the revenue a tariff would generate
A subsidy supports domestic production without distorting the prices that consumers face
Tariffs are always prohibited under WTO rules while subsidies are fully permitted
There is no economic difference; both policies have exactly identical welfare effects
questions.explanation: Both create production distortions, but a tariff also raises consumer prices (consumption distortion). A subsidy supports domestic output while consumers still pay the lower world price.

questions.question: According to the political economy of trade policy, why does protectionism often persist despite harming the majority of consumers?
options: The median voter is usually a worker in a protected import-competing industry
The benefits of protection are concentrated on a few while the costs are diffused across millions
Consumers do not participate in elections while producer groups vote at very high rates
International treaties require all countries to maintain a minimum level of trade protection
questions.explanation: A small group of producers each gains substantially from protection and has strong incentive to lobby. Millions of consumers each lose only a small amount and have little incentive to organize politically.

questions.question: What is the key institutional difference between a Customs Union and a Free Trade Area?
options: A Customs Union allows free movement of labor while a Free Trade Area does not
A Customs Union adopts a common external tariff that applies to all non-member countries
A Free Trade Area requires a common currency while a Customs Union does not need one
There is no meaningful institutional difference between these two arrangements
questions.explanation: In an FTA, each member maintains its own external tariffs. In a Customs Union, members harmonize their trade policy toward the rest of the world with a unified tariff wall.

questions.question: A large country imposes an import tariff. What happens to the world price of the imported good?
options: The world price increases because the tariff makes the good more expensive globally
The world price decreases because reduced demand forces foreign suppliers to lower prices
The world price remains unchanged because only domestic prices are affected by tariffs
The world price fluctuates unpredictably depending on exchange rate movements
questions.explanation: The large country's reduced import demand shifts the global demand curve leftward, forcing foreign exporters to accept lower pre-tariff prices to clear their inventory.

questions.question: What two types of efficiency loss constitute the deadweight loss from a tariff?
options: Government revenue loss and the reduction in foreign producer surplus abroad
Production distortion from inefficient domestic output and consumption distortion from reduced purchases
Administrative costs of customs enforcement and compliance expenses for all importers
Exchange rate depreciation effects and the resulting increase in all import prices
questions.explanation: The production distortion triangle captures the excess cost of domestic production replacing cheaper imports. The consumption distortion triangle captures the lost consumer surplus from reduced purchases.

questions.question: What does an escalating tariff structure refer to in international trade policy?
options: Tariff rates that increase automatically each year based on a pre-set schedule
Higher tariff rates applied to finished goods than to the raw materials used to make them
A cycle of retaliatory tariff increases between two competing trading partner nations
Tariffs that escalate from a low base rate as the total volume of imports increases
questions.explanation: Tariff escalation protects domestic processing and manufacturing. Raw materials enter at low tariffs, while finished goods face higher tariffs, magnifying effective protection for domestic industry.

questions.question: Which area on a standard small-country partial equilibrium diagram represents the government's tariff revenue?
options: The triangle representing the deadweight loss from distorted production decisions
The rectangle formed by the per-unit tariff rate times the quantity of imports after the tariff
The trapezoid capturing the transfer of surplus from consumers to domestic producers
The area between the domestic demand curve and the world price plus tariff line
questions.explanation: Government revenue = Tariff × Quantity of imports. This is represented by a rectangle with height equal to the per-unit tariff and width equal to post-tariff import volume.

questions.question: Under a Voluntary Export Restraint, which party typically captures the quota rent created by the trade restriction?
options: The importing country's government through higher customs duties and tax collection
Foreign producers, because they administer the export limit and can charge higher prices
Domestic consumers in the importing country through lower final retail prices
The quota rent is evenly shared between the importing and exporting country governments
questions.explanation: Because the exporting country controls the supply restriction, its firms can raise prices in the importing market and capture the resulting scarcity rent as additional profit.

questions.question: Which trade policy instrument is generally considered the most transparent form of protection?
options: A complex system of import quotas allocated to different supplying countries
A tariff, because it is an explicit tax with a clearly published percentage or dollar rate
Technical regulations and product safety standards applied to all imported goods
Government procurement policies that favor domestic suppliers over foreign bidders
questions.explanation: Tariffs are explicit taxes with clearly stated rates. Their protective effect is relatively easy to measure compared to quotas, technical barriers, or regulatory restrictions.

questions.question: What is a hidden cost of an import quota compared to an equivalent tariff?
options: Quotas always generate less government revenue than the equivalent tariff does
A quota can foster domestic monopolies because imports cannot expand even if the domestic firm raises prices
Tariffs are always less restrictive than quotas at the same nominal rate of protection
Quotas eliminate all foreign competition while tariffs still allow some imports to enter
questions.explanation: Under a tariff, import competition caps prices at world price plus tariff. Under a binding quota, once the limit is reached, the domestic firm faces no further competition and can fully exploit its market power.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of imposing a trade embargo on another country?
options: To protect domestic infant industries from being overwhelmed by foreign competition
To inflict economic pain on a target country as a tool of foreign policy and political leverage
To raise government revenue from the limited trade that continues under the embargo
To comply with World Trade Organization rules that require embargoes in certain situations
questions.explanation: Trade embargoes are primarily instruments of foreign policy and national security, not standard tools of economic efficiency or industrial protection.

questions.question: A country's Current Account and Capital Account together sum to –500. Assuming no Net Errors and Omissions, what must the Financial Account balance equal?
options: –500, mirroring the combined deficit in the other two accounts
+500, because all Balance of Payments accounts must together sum to exactly zero
0, because the Financial Account is independent of the other two accounts
+1,000, reflecting double the combined deficit for full accounting balance
questions.explanation: The Balance of Payments identity: $CA + KA + FA = 0$. If $CA + KA = -500$, then $FA = +500$ to bring the total to zero.

questions.question: What does a country's Net International Investment Position measure?
options: The annual flow of foreign direct investment moving into and out of the country
The stock of foreign assets owned by residents minus domestic assets owned by foreigners
The difference between the country's exports and imports of investment-related services
The total value of the central bank's official foreign currency reserve holdings
questions.explanation: NIIP is a stock measure capturing a country's net external wealth at a point in time: domestically owned foreign assets minus foreign-owned domestic assets.

questions.question: What is a sudden stop in the context of international finance?
options: A temporary trading halt imposed by regulators on all foreign exchange markets
A rapid and unexpected cessation of private capital inflows into a national economy
The gradual long-term decline of foreign investment over several business cycles
A government decision to suspend all imports of foreign financial services
questions.explanation: A sudden stop forces an immediate and often painful current account adjustment, as the country can no longer finance its external deficit through foreign borrowing.

questions.question: A country has a government budget surplus ($T > G$) and private saving exactly equals private investment ($S_p = I$). What is the state of its Current Account?
options: The Current Account must be in deficit because the government is running a surplus
The Current Account must be in surplus because $CA = (S_p - I) + (T - G) > 0$
The Current Account must be exactly zero because saving equals investment
The Current Account balance cannot be determined from this information alone
questions.explanation: $CA = (S_p - I) + (T - G) = 0 + (\text{positive}) = \text{positive}$. The government surplus creates a Current Account surplus.

questions.question: What does Relative Purchasing Power Parity predict about the relationship between exchange rates and inflation?
options: The level of the exchange rate equals the ratio of absolute price levels in the two countries
The percentage change in the exchange rate equals the difference in national inflation rates
Exchange rates are completely unrelated to inflation differentials between any two countries
Inflation always causes a currency to appreciate, not depreciate, in the long run
questions.explanation: Relative PPP states that $\%\Delta E = \pi_{domestic} - \pi_{foreign}$. The exchange rate adjusts to offset the inflation differential between two countries over time.

questions.question: When does an arbitrage opportunity exist in the foreign exchange market?
options: When all currency exchange rates are perfectly aligned with purchasing power parity
When the exchange rates quoted by different banks do not satisfy the cross-rate consistency condition
When central banks intervene simultaneously in multiple foreign exchange markets
Arbitrage opportunities cannot exist because forex markets are always perfectly efficient
questions.explanation: If the implied cross-rate from two currency pairs differs from the directly quoted rate for the third pair, a risk-free profit can be made by trading through the cheaper path until prices realign.

questions.question: What is the exchange rate overshooting model's explanation for short-run exchange rate volatility?
options: Central banks deliberately push exchange rates beyond their fundamental equilibrium values
Goods prices are sticky in the short run, so financial markets and exchange rates must adjust much more sharply
Speculative attacks by currency traders cause exchange rates to permanently overshoot equilibrium
The model predicts that exchange rates never deviate from their long-run equilibrium values
questions.explanation: Because goods prices adjust slowly but asset markets react instantly, the exchange rate must bear the full burden of adjustment to monetary shocks in the short run, causing it to overshoot its long-run level.

questions.question: What defines a currency board as an exchange rate arrangement?
options: A floating rate where the central bank sets only a long-run inflation target for the economy
The domestic currency is fully backed by foreign reserves at a permanently fixed exchange rate
A system where a committee of commercial bank executives meets to set the official rate weekly
Multiple countries sharing a single common currency managed by a supranational central bank
questions.explanation: A currency board is the strictest form of a fixed exchange rate. Every unit of domestic currency must be 100% backed by holdings of a foreign reserve currency, eliminating discretionary monetary policy.
